channel.title: IP on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in IP on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Resurrecting The Arcanum IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about resurrecting the Arcanum intellectual property.

I get asked a lot on this channel in the comment section about making more Arcanum. And I have videos on doing a remake of Arcanum, You know, bringing it up to modern standards — hardware code, UI. There’s a video on.

I’ll link it below. I’ve also talked about remastering Arcanum. I’ve also talked about which.

item.title: Why Not Create A New IP Today?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. It’s a fun Friday, so I want to tell you a story called.

Why Are You Not Creating a New IP today? It’s a fun story and I will get to it. Let me give you some background.

I’ve created a lot of new IPs from scratch: Fallout, Arcanum, Wildstar, Pillars, Tyranny, The Outer Worlds. In all of those I was a team. So it was a whole bunch of people came together to make those.

item.title: Planning For A Sequel
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about planning for a sequel.

Okay, sorry about that. I » got a new microphone there. My voice is normal again, It’s got voice modulation and a bunch of fun stuff. It was a birthday gift from Robert, so I I won’t do that again, but I just want to have fun. And, yes, this video is going up on my birthday because the planning for a sequel question came from a master member. So it’s getting snuck into the queue, Otherwise it would have shown up in October, Anyway, matt P asked: “A new studio is building their first RPG with a brand new IP.

item.title: Sci-Fi vs. High Fantasy Settings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about sci-fi versus high F fantasy settings, specifically in a new IP.

This comes from a question from Hungry Hungry Hobo 196, who asked: hey, Tim, if you had your choice to make a new crpg, would you rather do a high fantasy or sci-fi? How do you feel about sci-fi with fantasy elements such as magic, and how hard is it to make a game that has guns or lasers and make a sword feel viable? Those are good questions and once again I find myself going: haven’t I already talked about that and I looked, and I haven’t talked about it in one of my videos, but this is a perfect example of the kind of discussions I’ve had with many other game developers. We talk about things like this all the time, and what’s fun about talking with your fellow game developers is you’re not trying to con like someone, like a publisher or someone above you, to make a game this could be like. A lunchtime thing where you’re just sitting around talking and you’re like, yeah, you know what I prefer between these two and here’s why. And then there’s a spirited discussion of the topic and no one’s necessarily trying to convince anyone, you’re just trying to go. Well, here’s what I think blah. I love discussions like that because it helps me kind of hash out my own feelings on the topic.

item.title: Adapting TTRPGs vs. Other IPs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about adapting tabletop role playing games into video games versus adapting a non-game IP into a video game.

I think the into the video game part should probably be implicit, because I’ve never adapted anything into anything else but a video game. So let’s talk about the pros and cons of having a tabletop RPG versus. Is a non-game RPG a non-game IP? This is an answer to a question from Dr John wubber, who asked: I’m curious, since you did adaptations of d and d and vampire, the Masquerade, but also South Park, what kinds of challenges did you find making a game based on another type of game versus making a game based on something non — interactive like a TV show? That is a really interesting question and no one has ever asked that before. It’s, by the way, that’s if you’re wondering, like what you can do. That’s a John’s question — is really interesting to me because it’s like: oh, I’ve literally done a video.

item.title: Working In Another's Game IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about working in another’s game IP.

This is not me talking about work making a non, taking a non-game IP and turning it into a game. I have a whole video on that. I’m talking about you’re making a game in someone else’s Game World — maybe it’s a, tabletop, and you’re converting it to a video game, which is a slight change of medium. Or maybe you’re making a sequel in someone else’s game world. Or maybe you’re working in a game where someone else is doing most of the design work. I want to talk about that because it’s an interesting feeling and an interesting process to be involved in something like that. But before I do, I want to tell you guys a story. It wouldn’t be a kanong games video, would it, without a story.

item.title: My Games As Other Genres
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about modeling, constipation in role playing games e.

No, I’m not. Today I’m going to talk about realizing my games into other genres. This is something that I mentioned weeks ago in a video I made that was a postmortem of all my games combined together. It was called meta postmortem.

In the comment section of that video a lot of people mentioned that they wanted me to continue my thought on. I have a lot of ideas in my idea book about the IPS I’ve made, but making games with them that just weren’t role playing games, CU, I got a little, I said, I felt a little pigeon hold into making role playing games, not that I don’t like making them and I don’t have fun making them, but some of my IPS I thought would work better in other genres or just as well, and even though I don’t have any experience making them, I was like one day I wanted to have made them like that but I didn’t. I even got a request from someone in the business saying, hey, keep going with that thought. So this video is me keeping going with that thought.

item.title: Using An IP In A Different Medium
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about using an IP in a different medium, and

this is what I mean specifically is by IP is a license to an intellectual property. Maybe it was a tabletop role playing game, maybe it’s a book, maybe it’s a movie. For me, it would be making a game from that, and I have a whole video on converting movies into games, and there’s a link below, but it’s a similar process. This, using an IP in a different medium, is a similar process that TV shows or movies go through when they try to convert a book or game into their medium. And I’m not talking about the Fallout TV show here.

item.title: IP Idea: Unknown Powers
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about an IP idea that I had for unknown Powers.

This is Friday, funay. I thought I’d throw out another idea that’s in my idea book and it’s something that’s bugged me for a while — for years actually — that many games that are in the magic or superhero or Supernatural game genre they just explain powers in like mundane, excruciating detail, as if your character was born with a manual and just goes: oh, I can fly 60 feet a second or something. And that takes away a lot of the — at least for me, a lot of the Mystique and weirdness and non scientificness of what’s going on. And I understand why tabletop games have to do this, but computer games don’t. The computer can understand exactly what you have without telling you. So that’s what I want to talk about. What if you made a game in one of those genres where you have a random power assigned to you when you make a? New game but you can’t use it right away, you it turns on at a random after a random time interval, or an event happens to you and I’ll explain all that, and then it only works under certain conditions.

item.title: IP Idea: Magic Costs Dearly
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about another IP idea from my idea book.

Although I don’t think it’s in this one, I think it’s in one in my bookcase. This is the idea that magic should cost the Caster dearly. Now, this is an idea that’s been in my book since troa did the Lord of the Rings demo back in 2001, and some of you may already see what the connection is and I’ll talk about it when I when I get there. But what made me think about this IP idea was most fantasy RPGs have reduced magic to something almost mundane. It’s no different than a fighter swinging a sword or a thief picking a lock. It’s just a thing that someone does and it usually is just an excuse to have a different damage type and some particle effects. It’s — it’s explained away as in know magic, and I always thought that that was something that missed out a lot on what could be done with magic. And, to be fair, um, this has been like this since the original DN D. I mentioned that my original D and campaign was based on Roberty Howard’s Conan campaign, but that campaign was fairly low magic.

item.title: Ideas For A Generic Fantasy RPG
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about some ideas for a generic fantasy RPG.

Now I’ve already done some videos where I’ve talked my way through some notes I had on very specific IPS. These are IPS with you know, like I’ve always talked about, with defined settings and then a story, and then I’ve hinted at the mechanics I want to do.

But I found a document deep, in my archives. This one, this, the source of this document is troa in the early 2000s.

item.title: Relinquishing Creative Control
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about relinquishing creative control.

I’m going to answer a question from mjy media who asks: hey, Tim question, do you have tips on relinquishing creative control? I wrote a ttrpg book a couple years ago and handing off my writing to artists was the hardest thing to do. I had so many notes but I didn’t want to smother the artists. Some artist art didn’t turn out true to my vision and I’m so somewhat unhappy with around 20% of the art. I’m writing another book Now. Tips for this next round.

item.title: Another New IP Idea
item.description:
Hi everyone, this is Tim. Today I want to talk about another new IP idea.

I really had a fun time, um, in a video and I’ll link it below the video I did, uh, few weeks ago, where I talked about my idea for a new post aoch game where you played a little kid who survived some kind of Apocalypse, but and I had fun talking about that, but that was mostly setting and story. So I dug into my book and I found another game idea that I liked, that I never did anything with, so I’m going to throw it out here. And what’s different about this one is the setting is somewhat standard, but the it has a uni, unique mechanic that I think would make a really cool hook. Um, at least I think it’s Unique. I don’t know of any game that does this, so let’s talk about it. So this game is would be a fairly standard Fantasy game. Think, you know, Temple of Elemental Evil, think Arcanum without the Industrial Revolution. Think Lord of the Rings, think just a very standard Fantasy game.

item.title: Prequels
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about prequels now I did a video on sequels and it’s linked below.

But since I did that, I’ve had a number of people ask me about whether I would like to do a prequel, whether what I think of prequels. Um, and I think it was memes Lich who was the first one who asked, I kind of had to dive back through the comments and he asked: what do you feel about pre prequels, Tim? Let me give you the TLD DW, since I think a lot of people DW. Um, my feelings on prequels are very similar to my feelings on sequels, but I do like them more than doing a sequel. I mean I’m not going to, you know, change my tune here.

item.title: Player Expectations
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about player expectations.

What I mean by that is, when you’re making a game, you have to somehow convey to people what this game is going to be, and they’re going to form expectations of what they’re going to get, and that is really really tough to manage. And I’m going to talk about, like, how you go about trying to do it, what some of the problems are and, uh, why sometimes new IP makes it easier and sometimes it makes it harder. So — and yes, I apologize, the dog is at my feet and snoring really loudly in this video, um, so let’s talk about how you try to manage player expectations. Until the game ships, uh, or get close to shipping, you will use, probably, interviews. Uh, marketing tends not to be done till later, um, but, interview interviews are a very narrow bandwidth, it’s. You know people want to come and talk to you about your game, but frequently they want to talk to you about other things too.

item.title: An Example Of Making A New IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about making an IP an actual example.

Like I’ve talked about making and maintaining an IP, I’ve talked about the order to do it, in which is setting, story, system mechanics. I’ve talked about making what goes into a good setting. I’ve even talked about what your design doc should look like. Those are all videos I’ll link below.

Today, let’s do it. Let’s actually take something from one of my design idea books. I will talk about it, I will expound on it, I’ll show how the idea gets converted into an actual design that you can say now it’s an IP. So, and I will do it in the recommended order, talk about setting, story and systems. So my idea from this book was: I wanted to make a post-apocalyptic game — yeah, another post-apocalyptic game, but I didn’t want it to be nuclear war or any of the other things I’d use before. I had a new idea and what was going to be. Different about this one is: uh, a lot of people are gone, a lot of monsters, and I wanted to come up with more variation than what I got to do on Fallout, which was: here are some gos and giant bugs and mutants.

item.title: Thoughts On A Temple Remake
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my thoughts on a temple remake, a remake of Temple of Elemental Evil, with things I would like to do that either I ran out of time to do or wish

I had done differently. Um, I’ve already talked about my thoughts on a Fallout remake — that was months ago and an Arcanum remake yesterday, so I thought I’d do a temple remake. I’ll link those other remakes below. I’m not going to do a remake video for vampire Bloodlines, though it’s not really mine to remake thoughts from the about. A remake on that should come from Leonard bski or Jason Anderson. So I’m just GNA complete my Trio of remake Thoughts with temple today. So let’s get started. I the first thing I’d do just the standard.

item.title: Thoughts On An Arcanum Remake
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about thoughts on an Arcanum remake.

Uh, I’ve had different questions about it I even touched upon this a little bit in the video I did on Arcanum as a Windows 10 app, where I actually have Arcanum up and running in Windows 10, and I talked about some things I might do about it, but I didn’t finish the thought. I didn’t do it as completely as I did say in the video called thoughts on a Fallout remake. So I’ll link those two videos below, but I want to do this one, which it will kind of collect all my thoughts on.

item.title: Making And Maintaining An IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about making and maintaining an IP.

By IP, I mean intellectual property, now I’ve talked about writing design docs and how to make a good setting, so I’m going to link those two videos below. This is something different. I want to talk about intellectual property in general. Intellectual property is more than your setting. It’s more than your story. Those are specific to a game.

item.title: Thoughts On A Fallout Remake
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer a question from Evan shantlin Who, full disclosure, worked on QA on Fallout.

And Evan asks: hey, Tim, ever thought about what changes or updates you’d like to see in a Fallout remake? Would you want a fully explorable map or Base building added to the game? Are there errors you’d like to see completely reworked or just flushed out?

item.title: Troika's Post Apocalyptic Demo
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim.

What you are seeing is the only known video footage of epic, which was troika’s in-house post-apocalyptic game we started designing and implementing towards the end of vampire. Uh, of course it never came out. This footage isn’t from mine.

I’ll include the link below. It’s not from my archive. I don’t have any video footage. In fact, I don’t even have any code.

I have a lot of design docs and notes, but my epic folder seems very sparse. The first document I have for this game is dated January of 2003.. And the latest one is dated June of 2004., so let me tell you how I remember this, and I’ll also tell you one thing that someone at least disagrees with me on. But so in 2003 we were contacted by interplay who told us that Bethesda was bidding on the Fallout IP. And did we want to bid? We did. This is where our memories diverge.
